cards.definition: The earliest era of mass-market coffee (Folgers, Mr. Coffee) treated as a commodity — cheap, convenient, grab-and-go, with little attention paid to quality or origin.
cards.analogy: Coffee as fuel, like a gas-station fill-up rather than a meal.
cards.why_it_matters: Knowing this baseline helps you recognize how far "just a cup of coffee" has evolved — useful context whenever a café markets itself as different from supermarket coffee.
cards.cloze: The ___ of coffee treated it as a cheap, mass-market commodity with little focus on quality — think Folgers or an old drip machine.

cards.definition: The era defined by Starbucks-style espresso culture — customizable drinks, coffee shops as a social "third place," and the rise of the barista as a public-facing role.
cards.analogy: Coffee becomes an experience and a customizable order, not just fuel.
cards.why_it_matters: This is the wave most people still picture when they think "coffee shop" — recognizing it helps you spot when a café is actually operating in third-wave territory instead.
cards.cloze: ___ coffee is marked by Starbucks-style espresso culture, drink customization, and cafés as a social gathering spot.

cards.definition: The current era treating coffee as a craft — full traceability to origin, labels detailing altitude/variety/processing, and pride in brewing technique.
cards.analogy: Coffee treated more like wine or craft beer than a commodity.
cards.why_it_matters: This is the frame "specialty coffee" sits inside — useful shorthand whenever you're trying to explain why a bag of coffee costs more than the supermarket tin.
cards.cloze: ___ coffee treats the drink as a craft, with full traceability and labels detailing altitude, variety, and processing.
